description:
You are acting as a platform-engineering colleague who has been asked to set up a very small,
    repeatable performance-benchmark “smoke test” that will later run in a CI pipeline.  
    The purpose is to compare how long three common compressors take to compress the **same**
    10 MiB input file and to collect the most important metrics in a single CSV artefact that the
    pipeline can archive.

    1. Create a new directory  
       /home/user/benchmarks

    2. Inside that directory generate **exactly** one binary file called  
       /home/user/benchmarks/testfile.bin  
       – the file must be **exactly 10 MiB (10 × 1024 × 1024 = 10 , 485 , 760 bytes)** in size.  
       – Its contents do not have to be random, but they must be non-empty (all-zero is OK).

    3. Time how long each of the following compressors takes to create a compressed
       version of that file and keep the resulting compressed files in the same directory
       (names must match exactly):

           • gzip   => testfile.bin.gz
           • bzip2  => testfile.bin.bz2
           • xz     => testfile.bin.xz

    4. After running the three commands, create one single CSV summary file  
       /home/user/benchmarks/compression_benchmark.csv  
       with **precisely** the following properties so that an automated grader can parse it:

           a. It must contain **four lines total**: one header line + one data line per algorithm.
           b. The header line must be **exactly** (no spaces before/after the commas):

              algorithm,original_size_bytes,compressed_size_bytes,compression_ratio,time_seconds

              – “compression_ratio” = original_size_bytes ÷ compressed_size_bytes  
                (round **down** to two decimal places).  
              – “time_seconds” must be measured with a resolution of at least 1/100 s and use
                a dot as decimal separator (e.g. “0.14”, “2.03”).
           c. The three data lines can appear in any order but:
                • column 1 must be one of: gzip, bzip2, xz
                • column 2 must always be 10485760
                • column 3 must be the size in bytes of the respective compressed file
                • column 4 must be the computed ratio, two decimal places
                • column 5 must be the elapsed time for that compression run, two decimal places

           Example of **valid** CSV content (sizes and times will differ in your run):

              algorithm,original_size_bytes,compressed_size_bytes,compression_ratio,time_seconds
              gzip,10485760,324587,32.29,0.12
              bzip2,10485760,291004,36.03,0.87
              xz,10485760,259216,40.45,1.65

           The grader will verify:
              • File exists at the exact path.
              • Header line matches *exactly*.
              • There are exactly three data rows.
              • Column 2 for every row is 10485760.
              • Each column is comma-separated with no extra whitespace.

    5. Leave all intermediate files in place; the pipeline will clean them up later.

    Notes & Hints (do not paste these literally):
        • You can use “dd” or “truncate” to make the 10 MiB file.
        • “/usr/bin/time -f '%e' …” or “date +%s.%N” snapshots are acceptable for timing.
        • Remember to round the ratio and time to two decimal places before writing the CSV.
        • No super-user privileges are available; stay inside /home/user.

    When you are done, verify the CSV looks correct by printing its contents to the terminal.
truth:
<!--
    Ground-truth values that the automated test will look for.
    DO NOT reveal these in the public task description.
    -->
    <expected>
        <directory>/home/user/benchmarks</directory>
        <input-file>
            <path>/home/user/benchmarks/testfile.bin</path>
            <size-bytes>10485760</size-bytes>
        </input-file>
        <compressed-files>
            <file>testfile.bin.gz</file>
            <file>testfile.bin.bz2</file>
            <file>testfile.bin.xz</file>
        </compressed-files>
        <csv>
            <path>/home/user/benchmarks/compression_benchmark.csv</path>
            <header>algorithm,original_size_bytes,compressed_size_bytes,compression_ratio,time_seconds</header>
            <data-rows>3</data-rows>
            <original-size-field>10485760</original-size-field>
        </csv>
    </expected>
name: task_000000_1f60d8a3
